content.assessment: This proposal accepts the current default closure trajectory and centralized authority structure largely as given, focusing institutional change on transport-responsibility clarification and compensatory support rather than on the closure decision-making mechanism itself. It leaves tankerületi centers' authority over closure decisions and their use of efficiency/enrollment criteria fully intact, meaning the institutional question of who decides closure is not engaged; the proposal operates entirely downstream of that decision. Given that 38% of settlements already lack public educational institutions, a default-closure model applied without addressing the sorting effects documented for enrollment-threshold decisions risks compounding existing territorial coverage gaps.
opportunities: Could interact constructively with the 2026 reform if transport-responsibility clarification is folded into that broader restructuring vehicle
strengths: Does not require restructuring the centralized authority system, making it administratively simpler to implement within existing governance
threats: Applying enrollment-threshold-based default closure at scale, given that class-size minimums (14-27 pupils) and per-pupil funding formulas already structurally disadvantage small village schools, could accelerate closures beyond the current pattern without institutional counterweight
weaknesses: Leaves tankerületi centers' application of efficiency and enrollment criteria for closure fully unchanged, meaning the sorting mechanism that determines which schools close remains untouched by this proposal

Does not address the underlying territorial coverage problem (38% of settlements already without any public educational institution), since default closure under transparent thresholds could plausibly increase rather than reduce this gap over time
